data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook: The Financial Damage Of War
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Week in review – Oil Wrought Havoc on Markets

Now reaching the third week of the US-Iran-Israel conflict, ongoing disruptions to the energy commodity market are starting to pose grand problems.

In short, higher oil prices translate to higher inflation. Higher inflation translates to less profitability. Lower profitability and higher inflation translate into struggling markets.

Stock markets have taken a hit around the globe. Having remained impressively stable until recently, they are now entering a tougher period ahead.

As global oil supply finds itself in jeopardy, companies will face significantly higher energy prices in the coming period; this effect should last at least as long as the Strait of Hormuz remains in de facto closure (only a few boats and tankers, if any, are crossing every day).

The issue is that markets are starting to reprice the prolonged inflationary effect of such prices – the 1970s consecutive petrol crises taught painful lessons.

After the oil embargoes during that decade, stagflation brought heavy pain to consumers and global markets – and the effects lasted about 10 years, culminating in a significant economic cooldown in the early 80s.

With many ships attacked throughout the week, WTI went from about $80 to today's $98. This doesn't even mention the 30% up-and-down swings in WTI prices since Monday.

To put things into perspective, this is about 72% higher than the prices seen in early January. But the issue is that this only reflects crude barrels - refined petrol products have well exceeded this rise worldwide, particularly in Asia.

The issue is that the narrative is shifting from a 4-5-week short war to the actual pricing of a much longer, more painful conflict. The media is blaming the US government for its lack of "exit plans".

Hence, inflation expectations are racing higher, yields are rising above their 2026 peaks, and cuts are being priced out, replaced by hikes, making the overall situation look quite grim.

The US Fed Funds rate cut pricing went from close to 65 bps to the current below ~20 bps. President Trump is proving Powell right yet again.

The only one enjoying the ride is the petrodollar, which serves as the denominator for much more expensive crude and loves the pricing out of cuts. The issue is that, at the current point in the economic cycle, this could pose heavy restraints on the economy.

Tracking upcoming data (particularly labor and manufacturing activity) and next week's Fed communications will be essential to get a better idea of what's coming.

The USD correlation with oil rises is once again pretty evident. Hence, it will be essential for traders to monitor the Dollar Index's movements to track overall market flows in the coming period.

On a brighter note (if we can call it such; there's nothing really bright about war), the US and Israel are making some strong advances to their strategy, with 90% of drone and ballistic missile launchers destroyed, and similar damage to the IRGC's military production sites.

The idea is that if Iran doesn't want to throw in the towel, and the US and Israel insist on inflicting mortifying blows to its enemy, markets will have to count on the latter to hope for a shorter conflict.

To me, we are not at maximum market fear yet, but there is a high chance that fears will get overblown. This could provide decent opportunities in the coming weeks.

Oil and Strait of Hormuz developments are, for now, the two largest threats to general market pricing, so keep the two in check. (And don't forget about the private credit trouble lurking from afar.)

Weekly Performance across Asset Classes

Metals were the worst performers of this week's action, and by far. As long as oil maintains a higher path, traders can expect similar flows; that is, as long as Strait of Hormuz traffic doesn't pick up.

The Week Ahead – Major Central Bank decisions

Asia Pacific Markets – Royal Bank of Australia and Bank of Japan's Rate Decisions

APAC traders will see the release of China's industrial production numbers and retail sales, two key releases to track the second largest economy and particularly if the effect of their local supportive policies has been working.

This should have a slight influence on AUD prices, but some other releases should mark the strongest 2026 performance even more. Monday evening welcomes the Royal Bank of Australia's rate decision, when a hike is about 80% priced.

This would bring the Australian rate to 4.35%, largely the highest for major currencies (also erasing the 2025 very temporary rate cuts). Aussie dollar traders will also look for communication hints towards Wednesday's employment data for Australia.

Kiwi GDP data for 2025 will also finally be released, so that should also get the NZD in the spotlight. Thursday will bring the final major market catalyst, with the Bank of Japan's rate decision.

A hike is about 10% priced in for next week. A surprise hike would surely change a lot to the current yen weakness, but to me, it wouldn't be so uncalled for after recent JPY weakness. If the BoJ doesn't deliver, April will be the next rate hike target.

Europe and UK Markets – An intense Thursday Session

Europe will be mostly muted throughout next week, but that wouldn't include Thursday's triple threat. Currency traders will have a lot to work with between the UK Employment Data and, most importantly, the Swiss National Bank, Bank of England and ECB Rate Decisions, all in the same day (and in that order).

North American Markets – Bank of Canada and FOMC

Canada will open the week with its inflation data, but the largest day for North American markets will surely be Wednesday. The Bank of Canada and the FOMC will release their rate decisions, with no change expected.

Keep a very close eye and ear on what Powell has to say during his press conference. And don't forget the US PPI release!

Keep a close eye on geopolitical developments, particularly those involving the US-Iran talks, as they are likely to continue influencing commodity and broader markets.

Next Week's High-Tier Economic Events

March 13th Market Wrap

Today marked a decent rebound in cryptocurrencies, which really stands out from the recent market trends. Apart from that, oil bounces higher again despite its pullback attempt, and the US dollar found new highs against most of its FX peers, particularly Antipodeans and Europeans. The Dollar Index is now trading above its November peak.

In terms of economic data, releases were soft. Core PCE came in softer than expected (2.8% vs 2.9% exp.), University of Michigan barely moved (which is a good sign these days), and Canadian employment shrugged by 89.3K.

This could somewhat ease inflationary fears, but this would more likely have its effect on Monday (as weekend risk gets unrolled, if there are valid reasons to do so!)

Safe trades, and enjoy your weekend!

Original Post

data.title: Tracking Ron Baron's BAMCO Portfolio - Q4 2025 Update
data.description:
This article is part of a series that provides an ongoing analysis of the changes made to BAMCO Inc.’s 13F stock portfolio on a quarterly basis. It is based on Ron Baron’s regulatory 13F Form filed on 02/17/2026. The 13F portfolio value decreased from $37.71B to $36.91B this quarter. The holdings are diversified, with recent 13F reports showing around 350 positions. There are 32 securities that are significantly large (more than ~0.5% of the portfolio each), and they are the focus of this article. The largest five stakes are Tesla Motors, Arch Capital Group, MSCI Inc., CoStar Group, and IDEXX Labs. They add up to ~30% of the portfolio. Please visit our Tracking Ron Baron’s BAMCO Portfolio series to get an idea of their investment philosophy and our last update for the fund’s moves during Q3 2025.

Baron Capital currently has ~$50B under management. The assets are distributed among several mutual funds and ETFs with the common theme of long-term, research-driven growth investment solutions. Their flagship mutual funds are Barron Asset Fund (BARIX), incepted in 1987, and Baron Partners Fund (BPTIX), incepted in 1992. Both funds have produced significant alpha over their lifetimes –11.27% annualized for Baron Asset Fund compared to 10.45% for the Russell Midcap Growth and 15.93% annualized for Baron Partners Fund compared to 10.22% for the Russell Midcap Growth. In December 2025, they launched several actively managed ETFs – the flagship Baron First Principles ETF (RONB), Baron Global Durable Advantage ETF (BCGD), Baron SMID Cap ETF (BCSM), Baron Financials ETF (BCFN), and Baron Technology ETF (BCTK).

Note: Their Baron Partners Fund (BPTIX), Baron Focused Growth Fund (BFGFX), and the Baron First Principles ETF (RONB) ETF are ways for retail investors to get exposure to Elon Musk’s SpaceX (SPACE).

Stake Increases:

MSCI Inc. (MSCI): MSCI is 4.28% of the portfolio position established in 2007. Next year saw the stake built to a ~9.9M share position at prices between ~$12.50 and ~$36. The 2012-2014 timeframe saw the position reduced by ~75% at prices between ~$26 and ~$50. There was a ~10% stake increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$525 and ~$593. That was followed by another ~15% stake increase this quarter at prices between ~$525 and ~$594. The stock currently trades at ~$548.

CoStar Group (CSGP): CSGP is now at 3.55% of the portfolio. It is a very long-term position that has been in the portfolio for over two decades. The stake was first purchased in 2000, and the position remained very small for the next three years. The bulk of the current position was built in the 2003-2004 timeframe at prices between ~$1.75 and ~$4.70. 2014 also saw a ~45% stake increase at prices between $14.20 and $21.20. Since then, the position has remained relatively steady, although adjustments were made in most quarters. Recent activity follows. The two years through Q4 2020 saw a ~22% selling at prices between ~$29.50 and ~$94. The stock currently trades at $43.63. The last several quarters have seen only minor adjustments.

Note: Baron Capital owns ~5% of CoStar Group. The prices quoted above are adjusted for the 10-for-1 stock split in June 2021.

FactSet Research (FDS): FDS is a 2.27% long-term stake first purchased in 2006. The next two years saw a 4.7M share stake built at prices between $34 and $69. The position has since been reduced through minor selling almost every quarter. The last quarter saw a ~20% selling at prices between ~$282 and ~$453. There was a ~60% stake increase this quarter at prices between ~$251 and ~$306. The stake is now at ~2.9M shares, and the stock is at ~$206.

Note: Baron Capital owns ~7.5% of FactSet Research.

Choice Hotels International (CHH): CHH already had a huge 19.4M share position in their first 13F filing in Q1 1999. That original stake was reduced by ~90% to a ~1.9M share stake by 2004 at prices between ~$3 and $22. 2007 saw a ~70% stake increase at prices between ~$26.50 and ~$34. Since then, the position was relatively steady till 2014. The five years through 2019 saw minor trimming in most quarters – overall, there was a ~15% selling during that period at prices between ~$42 and ~$105. There was a ~7% stake increase during Q2 2025 at prices between ~$116 and ~$134. That was followed by a similar increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$106 and ~$137. There was a ~40% stake increase this quarter at prices between ~$84 and ~$107. The stock currently trades at $95.46.

Note: Baron Capital owns ~16% of Choice Hotels.

Amazon.com (AMZN), Birkenstock Holdings plc (BIRK), FIGS Inc. (FIGS), Interactive Brokers (IBKR), Nvidia Corp (NVDA), On Holding (ONON), Red Rock Resorts (RRR), SiteOne Landscape (SITE), Spotify Technology (SPOT), Shopify (SHOP), Verisk Analytics (VRSK), and Welltower (WELL): These positions were increased during the quarter.

Note: Baron Capital has a ~20% ownership stake in Red Rock Resorts and a ~32% ownership stake in FIGS.

Stake Decreases:

Tesla Motors (TSLA): TSLA was a minutely small stake first purchased in 2012. The current position was built from 2014 at an average cost of ~$14.50 per share. The three quarters through Q1 2021 had seen a ~25% selling at prices between ~$148 and ~$294. Q2 2024 saw another ~10% trimming at prices between ~$142 and ~$198. The three quarters through Q1 2025 also saw minor trimming. There was a ~8% trimming during the next quarter at prices between ~$214 and ~$368. That was followed by a similar reduction during the last quarter at prices between ~$289 and ~$451. The stock currently trades at ~$391 and is still the largest 13F position by far at 14.53% of the portfolio. There was marginal trimming this quarter.

Note 1: In a CNBC interview in October 2021, it was revealed that their TSLA cost basis is ~$14.50 per share. They are very bullish and expect to hold long-term.

Note 2: The prices quoted above are adjusted for stock splits.

Arch Capital Group (ACGL): ACGL is a 4.67% of the portfolio stake first purchased in 2007 at prices between ~$7 and ~$8.25. The position remained relatively steady over the next three years. 2011 saw a ~17% stake increase at prices between ~$9.50 and ~$12.50, and that was followed with a similar increase in 2018 at prices between ~$25.50 and ~$31. The last several quarters saw minor trimming. The stock is now at $93.47.

Note: Baron Capital owns ~5% of Arch Capital Group.

IDEXX Labs (IDXX): IDXX is a 3.30% of the portfolio stake first purchased in 2005. The next three years saw a large stake build-up at prices between ~$15 and ~$30. The four years through Q1 2023 saw a ~40% selling. There was a ~7% trimming during the last quarter at prices between ~$507 and ~$688. The stock currently trades at ~$574. This quarter also saw a ~5% trimming.

Gartner Inc. (IT): IT is a 2.88% of the portfolio position first purchased in 2007 at prices between ~$16 and ~$28. The next three years saw consistent buying as the original position was increased by ~50% at prices between ~$9 and ~$28. The 2014-2015 timeframe saw a ~22% reduction at prices between ~$66 and ~$93. Recent activity follows: the seven quarters through Q3 2020 saw a ~20% selling at prices between ~$83 and ~$171. There was a ~18% stake increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$224 and ~$410. The stock currently trades at ~$167. This quarter saw a minor ~3% trimming.

Guidewire Software (GWRE): Guidewire had an IPO in January 2012. The 2.80% GWRE position was purchased in the 2012-2013 timeframe at prices between ~$20 and ~$49. 2017 also saw a ~25% stake increase at prices between ~$51 and ~$83. The stock currently trades at ~$161. The last few quarters saw minor trimming.

Note: Baron Capital owns ~6.3% of Guidewire Software.

Hyatt Hotels (H): The 2.65% Hyatt stake was established in the high-20s price range in 2009 following their IPO that November. The next five years saw the position almost double at prices between ~$29 and ~$64. 2016 saw a ~30% selling at prices between ~$37 and ~$58. Since then, the stake has remained relatively steady, although adjustments were made every quarter. There was a ~15% stake increase during Q3 2021 at prices between ~$69 and ~$81. The stock currently trades at ~$139. The last few quarters saw minor increases, while in this quarter there was marginal trimming.

Note: Baron Capital owns ~13% of Hyatt Hotels.

Kinsale Capital (KNSL): KNSL is a 1.89% of the portfolio stake. A very small position was established in 2016 following Kinsale’s IPO. Most of the current position was built the following year at prices between ~$28 and ~$45. The six quarters through Q2 2020 saw a ~30% stake increase at prices between ~$58 and ~$157. The stock currently trades at ~$363. There was a ~15% stake increase over the six quarters through Q2 2022, while the next ten quarters saw minor trimming. There was a marginal increase over the last three quarters, while in this quarter there was marginal trimming.

Note: Baron Capital owns ~7.6% of Kinsale Capital.

Vail Resorts (MTN): MTN is a 1.73% of the portfolio very long-term stake. During their first 13F filing in Q1 1999, the position was already very large at 11.6M shares. That original stake was sold down by around two-thirds in the 2003-2004 timeframe at prices between ~$11.50 and ~$23. Since then, there has only been minor activity. There was a ~16% reduction this quarter at prices between ~$133 and ~$163. The stock currently trades at ~$132.

Note: Baron Capital owns ~12% of Vail Resorts.

Mettler-Toledo International (MTD): MTD is a 0.55% of the portfolio position established during 2008 at prices between ~$68 and ~$110. The following year saw a ~150% stake increase at prices between ~$47 and ~$100. Since 2009, the position has seen consistent selling: ~93% reduction at prices between ~$100 and ~$1341. There was a ~30% further selling this quarter at prices between ~$1228 and ~$1525. The stock is now at ~$1174. They are harvesting long-term gains.

Amphenol Corp. (APH), CBRE Group (CBRE), Charles Schwab (SCHW), Houlihan Lokey (HLI), Morningstar Inc. (MORN), Primerica Inc. (PRI), and Vertiv Holdings (VRT): These positions were reduced during the quarter.

The spreadsheet below highlights changes to Ron Barron’s US stock holdings in Q4 2025:

Source: John Vincent. Data constructed from Ron Baron’s 13F filings for Q3 2025 and Q4 2025.

data.title: Imperial Petroleum: Decent Quarter But Uncertain Times Ahead - Hold
data.description:
Note:

I have covered Imperial Petroleum Inc. (IMPP, IMPPP) previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier articles on the company.

Earnings Review

Last week, Imperial Petroleum reported solid fourth-quarter results, with decent profitability and strong cash generation:

Revenues benefited from rising charter rates, strong operational utilization, and the recent addition of seven dry bulk carriers.

Adjusted EBITDA of $22.1 million and operating cash flow of $23.8 million represented new two-year highs.

Fleet Expansion Continues

Subsequent to quarter-end, the company took delivery of the Post-Panamax dry bulk carrier Post Marvel, thus increasing its fleet to 20 vessels with an estimated market value of $385 million:

Please note that Imperial Petroleum has agreed to acquire an additional six vessels from entities affiliated with CEO Harry Vafias for an aggregate purchase price of approximately $112 million.

The company ended the quarter with $179.1 million in cash and liquid investments, close to an $80 million quarter-over-quarter increase.

Surprise Equity Offering And Subsequent Share Repurchase Program

Remember that Imperial Petroleum raised approximately $56 million in net proceeds from a combined share and warrant offering in November.

The offering caught market participants flat-footed and resulted in the stock giving back most of its 2025 gains until early February:

On February 9, Imperial Petroleum stopped the bleeding by announcing a new $10 million share repurchase program. In subsequent weeks, the company utilized $0.9 million in cash to repurchase 0.25 million shares at an average price of $3.58.

Valuation - Cheap For Good Reason

Despite the recent recovery rally, Imperial Petroleum continues to trade at a steep discount to estimated net asset value ("NAV"):

Given the company's history of pursuing growth at the expense of common shareholders and considering the material warrant overhang, I would expect the discount to persist for the time being.

Lifting the share price closer to NAV would likely require at least one of the following actions:

  • initiation of a generous quarterly dividend
  • more aggressive share repurchases
  • material insider buying in the open market

Outlook Amid Middle East Conflict

Unfortunately, the recent escalation in the Middle East is posing a threat to the global shipping industry, as soaring oil prices could push the world economy into recession.

Please note that increasing oil supply shortages are likely to negatively impact tanker charter rates sooner rather than later.

Investors looking for options to protect their shipping portfolios should consider a bet on oil via exchange-traded securities like, for example, the United States Oil Fund (USO) or the United States Brent Oil Fund (BNO).

Looking ahead to the first quarter, the dry bulk side of the business will be impacted by seasonality, while market disruptions should support tanker charter rates in the near term.

However, a persistent lack of crude oil supply from the Middle East would result in substantially reduced crude and product tanker demand over time.

On the flip side, a near-term reopening of the Strait of Hormuz would likely give tanker charter rates a nice boost.

With the situation remaining in flux, I am reiterating my "Hold" rating on the company's common shares for now.

Series A Preferred Shares - Income Vehicle

In contrast to common shareholders, holders of the company's 8.75% Series A Cumulative Redeemable Perpetual Preferred Shares (IMPPP) actually benefited from the surprise equity offering, as Imperial Petroleum's strengthened balance sheet and liquidity have reduced the risks associated with an investment even further.

The preferred shares are currently trading slightly above their $25 liquidation preference, thus resulting in a dividend yield of approximately 8.3%, which I would consider safe for the time being.

Please note that the company has the option to call the preferred shares at any time, which would result in a small capital loss for investors at current prices.

In addition, there are a number of higher-yielding preferred shares in the shipping space that have been issued by more shareholder-friendly companies:

However, I still consider the company's preferred shares a solid "Hold" position.

Bottom Line

Imperial Petroleum reported another good quarter, with results boosted by favorable charter rates and strong fleet utilization.

Thanks to the recently announced repurchase program, the company's shares have partially recovered from the selloff caused by a surprise equity offering in November.

While Imperial Petroleum's common shares continue to change hands well below net asset value, I would expect the discount to persist for the time being as investors are likely to remain wary of further shareholder-unfriendly moves.

In addition, the recent Middle East conflict escalation has the potential to negatively impact shipping demand quite meaningfully over time.

With the situation remaining in flux, I am reiterating my "Hold" rating on the company's common shares for now.

While I see no near-term risk for Imperial Petroleum's preferred stock dividend, there are a number of higher-yielding alternatives in the shipping space.

As a result, I am reiterating my "Hold" rating on the company's 8.75% Series A Cumulative Redeemable Perpetual Preferred Shares.

data.title: Slow Growth Keeps Me On The Sidelines For First Hawaiian
data.description:
This article serves as a follow-up to a previous analysis for First Hawaiian, Inc. (FHB) that was published on October 29 of last year. At that time, I rated its stock as a Hold, an assessment that has matched its performance over the last four and a half months. FHB has lost 4% of its stock price during this time, faring slightly worse than the overall market. However, with dividends included, the stock is just over 1% ahead of the S&P 500.

The company released its Q4 and FY 2025 financial results back on January 30. The GAAP profit of $0.56 per share was one cent ahead of expectations, but three cents behind Q3 2025. Other performance metrics that are important for regional banks were mostly good, but there were a few areas where FHB took a step back at the end of last year. I am maintaining my Hold position for First Hawaiian, given that the company may find it challenging to show meaningful positive improvements in the first half of 2026.

Company Overview

First Hawaiian is the holding company for First Hawaiian Bank. The company has a history that dates back over 160 years, but it did not start using the “First Hawaiian” name until 1969. In 2003, it became the largest bank in its home state in terms of total assets. It completed its initial public offering on the NASDAQ in August of 2016.

Today, the bank controls 49 total branches, 45 in Hawaii, three in Guam, and one in Saipan. Its assets have reached $24 billion, making it the 81st largest bank in the US, according to the most current information from the Federal Reserve. An overview of the bank’s service area and key financial data can be seen in the image below.

FHB has a conservative loan-to-deposit ratio of 69.8%. As part of its 2025 10-K filing, the company reported $14.3 billion in total loans and leases as of December 31, 2025. That total represented an increase of 1.4% from Q3 2025 but a decrease of 0.7% from the end of 2024. At the end of Q4, First Hawaiian also controlled investment securities valued at $5.6 billion. Almost $1 billion of the company’s assets, just under 4.2%, are classified as goodwill.

Further details about the bank’s loan mix can be seen below. The largest allocation belongs to commercial real estate and construction loans at $5.4 billion, or 37.8% of the total. Residential mortgages are second in weight for FHB at 28.7%. The only other segment with an allocation of over 10% is commercial and industrial loans, which total $2.2 billion, or 15.4%.

Deposits at the bank totaled $20.5 billion at the end of Q4 2025. That was a decrease of about $200 million from Q3 but an increase of that same amount from the end of the previous year. FHB has a high percentage of demand deposits that earn no interest, which helps the bank maintain a low overall cost of deposits. According to the FDIC, the Q4 2025 national average for deposit costs for banks with similar assets was 2.12%, 83 basis points higher than the number for First Hawaiian.

FHB has a forward dividend yield of 4.36%, well above the sector median of 3.10%. Dividend growth is not a strength for the stock, however, as its quarterly payout has not increased since Q1 2019. The company did return additional capital to its shareholders through a stock repurchase plan last year, buying back about one million shares in Q4. Starting in FY 2026, the company has already been authorized to repurchase an additional $250 million in stock, a total that represents about 8.5% of the company’s current market cap of $2.93 billion.

Analysis of First Hawaiian

A snapshot of First Hawaiian’s performance from Q4 2025 can be seen below. As referenced in my introduction, net income and diluted earnings per share declined slightly in the most recent three-month period. At the same time, the company did show a two-basis-point improvement in net interest margin and efficiency ratio. The net interest margin of 3.21% is surprisingly 68 basis points below the national average for a bank of its size. With the bank’s low cost of deposits, I would expect this number to be larger, but FHB also has a yield on its total loans that is 140 basis points below average.

Other notable changes from Q3 to Q4 last year were the slight declines in return on assets and return on equity. At 1.16%, the bank’s ROA is still relatively strong, but it dropped six basis points from Q3. ROE of 10.07% fell 74 basis points and now stands over 2% below the national mean.

While the asset quality for the loans held for investment for First Hawaiian appears to be healthy overall, I am always more hesitant to invest in regional banks when the trend is moving in the wrong direction. From Q1 to Q4 last year, the bank’s ratio of non-performing assets almost doubled from 0.17% to 0.31%. Net charge-offs have increased from 0.09% to 0.14% from Q2 to Q4. As a standalone statistic, neither of these ratios is cause for great alarm, but it is something to watch in the next quarter. The bank has increased its allowance for credit losses slightly to account for some of this concern.

If the valuation of First Hawaiian were lower, I could make a better argument to elevate its stock to a buy. However, slow earnings growth gives FHB a price-to-earnings growth ratio of almost two. I generally like for this number to be below one when recommending an investment in a small-cap bank. The book value per share for the stock is 22.57, which gives First Hawaiian a reasonable price-to-BV ratio of 1.06. However, the metric I prefer to use to analyze banks, its tangible book value per share, is 14.46, 35.9% lower than its book value. This gap is due to the sizeable amount of goodwill on the books. At a price to TBV ratio of 1.65, FHB no longer looks like a bargain. At the end of Q4, the mean price to TBV for a US bank was 1.40, according to S&P Global.

Risks to Consider

The health of the state economy in Hawaii is heavily dependent on government spending and tourism. Neither of these areas appears to be in a strong position at the start of this year. Government spending has been under pressure over the last 14 months, as there have now been three shutdowns of some type, either all or partial, since President Trump took office. As part of the company’s Q3 2025 investor conference call, First Hawaiian CEO Robert Harrison noted that the bank could see some impact from the shutdown of the federal government that lasted 43 days in Q4. That could explain some of the declining numbers mentioned earlier in this analysis. The most recent funding issues for the Department of Homeland Security have begun to impact air travel, which is not good news for a state that needs visitors to sustain its hospitality industry.

The current conflict in the Middle East has also impacted oil prices and caused airlines to raise their ticket prices while canceling other flights altogether. This adds further pressure to the tourism industry. Visitor spending is about 18% of the GDP in the state of Hawaii. Rising oil prices also put a dent in the wallets of local residents, as gas prices have risen to $4.69 per gallon in Hawaii. That is the third-highest rate in the US and $0.14 per gallon more than they were a week ago, on average. Prolonged inflation for energy costs could reduce consumers disposable income and have a ripple effect on local businesses.

Wall Street analysts are still quite pessimistic about the bank’s future, as four of the 10 analysts that cover FHB consider the stock to be a Sell or a Strong Sell. When I first wrote about the company last October, it had a short interest of 5.76%. As I write today, that number has risen to 7.42%, showing even greater bets that the stock will fall further from its current price of $23.95 per share.

The Bottom Line

As inflation nationwide appears to be more stubborn than ever and both businesses and consumers are feeling the impact of higher gas prices, I am becoming more selective on just what regional banks I consider to be a buy. There are certainly some positive characteristics for First Hawaiian, but the valuation already reflects most of those variables. Earnings growth that is predicted to be just 3% in FY 2026 and 6% in FY 2027 is not enough for me to believe that FHB will outperform the overall regional banking index moving forward. The dividend may be attractive enough to keep some income-oriented investors on board, but there is not enough in the overall picture for the company for me to change my original assessment. Therefore, I am maintaining my Hold rating on First Hawaiian.

data.title: This Week's Market Wrap: Strait To Jail
data.description:
By Ryan J. Puplava

The Strait of Hormuz Became the Market’s Fault Line

This week’s market action was not primarily about earnings, not primarily about the Fed, and not primarily about valuation. It was about war risk, energy supply, and whether one of the world’s most important maritime chokepoints could function normally. The conflict involving Iran escalated into the central macro driver for global markets because the Strait of Hormuz is not some secondary shipping lane.

It is the artery through which a massive share of the world’s oil moves, and once traders believed flows through that route were meaningfully impaired, asset prices across equities, energy, and rates had to reprice quickly. Reuters reported that the current disruption amounts to the largest oil supply shock on record, with roughly 8 million barrels per day affected, or about 8% of global supply.

That is why the tape looked so unstable all week. On Monday, crude initially surged as traders priced in the possibility of prolonged closure and supply interruption, then pulled back sharply when talk surfaced that the G7 would revisit a coordinated stockpile release and when President Trump suggested the war might end sooner than expected.

That drop in oil triggered a broad equity rebound and a drop in the Volatility Index. But the market never truly settled, because every apparent easing headline was met by another reminder that the physical security situation in and around Hormuz remained highly uncertain.

Reuters separately reported attacks on civilian vessels, mines being placed, projectiles striking ships, and a shipping environment in which merchant traffic is effectively being forced to navigate a live conflict zone.

The deeper issue is that this was not just a fear trade; it was a logistics trade. Reuters reported Saudi Arabia cut production by roughly 2 million barrels per day to around 8 million barrels per day after shutting major offshore fields, while broader Gulf region losses were estimated by the IEA at around 10 million barrels per day.

In other words, this was not merely about the price of oil futures reacting to dramatic headlines. It was about a real-world interruption to how oil is produced, moved, insured, and delivered. That helps explain why equity markets could rally on one headline and reverse hard on the next.

Investors were trying to decide whether they were seeing a temporary wartime premium or the early stage of a more durable energy shock that could bleed into inflation, margins, freight, and consumer demand.

Reuters reported the U.S. destroyed Iranian vessels accused of laying mines, while U.S. officials also faced public confusion over whether naval escorts through Hormuz were actually feasible in the near term. On one hand, the market wanted to believe U.S. military involvement could stabilize shipping.

On the other hand, Reuters reported the U.S. Navy told the shipping industry escorts were not possible for now, which undercut confidence that Washington could quickly normalize flows. That tension mattered greatly for markets. It meant U.S. force projection was large enough to raise the geopolitical stakes, but not yet credible enough to eliminate the supply-risk premium embedded in oil.

The week’s later headlines reinforced that fragility. Reuters reported Iran’s new supreme leader said the strait would remain shut, and a separate Reuters report said ships would have to coordinate with Iran’s navy to pass through the waterway.

Even where there were signs of partial passage by individual vessels, the broader message to markets was unmistakable: shipping through Hormuz had become conditional, dangerous, and politically weaponized. That is a completely different backdrop from a routine oil-market scare. It turns oil from a commodity story into a geopolitical leverage story, and markets typically assign a much higher premium to that kind of uncertainty.

Investment implication: When a geopolitical conflict directly impairs a critical global energy chokepoint, markets tend to trade less on fundamentals and more on the probability distribution of escalation, duration, and state intervention.

Goldman Sachs, via a Morningstar article, stated, “War fear goes beyond oil…every 10% rise in oil increases inflation by 0.2%”. The rise in oil could completely wipe away the benefits of the Big Beautiful Bill’s tax refunds expected this year, which hurts the consumer discretionary sector.

Markets Skeptical of Emergency Reserves

One of the most important developments of the week was the International Energy Agency’s decision to organize the largest emergency stock release in its history. The IEA said its 32 member countries unanimously agreed to make 400 million barrels from emergency reserves available to the market in response to disruptions caused by the Middle East conflict.

Reuters also reported that the agency viewed the current shock as the largest-ever supply disruption, which explains why such an extraordinary response was considered necessary.

And yet, despite that announcement, oil still rose again. That is the key point. Yes, Reuters reported IEA chief Fatih Birol said the release had a “strong impact” on markets, and there clearly was some immediate relief in pricing and risk sentiment after the measure was announced.

But by midweek and into the end of the week, crude had resumed climbing as traders concluded that reserve releases can buy time, not solve a chokepoint crisis while vessels are still under attack and maritime traffic remains constrained. Reuters reported oil settled up sharply on Thursday as tanker attacks continued and Iran maintained its position on Hormuz, even after the emergency stock release had already been set in motion.

That distinction matters. Emergency reserves are useful when the problem is a temporary mismatch between supply and demand, or when policymakers need to cushion a short-lived shock. They are less powerful when the market is asking whether a war can continue to physically impair transport routes for days or weeks.

In that environment, every barrel released from storage is competing with a more important question: can producers and shippers move fresh supply safely and consistently? This week, the market’s answer was not convincing enough to keep oil down. Reuters noted that despite coordinated action, Brent still climbed back above the psychologically important $100 area during the week, showing that traders viewed the reserve release as a mitigation tool rather than a cure.

The market also had to account for the signaling effect. The IEA’s decision was reassuring in one sense because it showed policymakers were willing to act aggressively. But it was also alarming in another sense because it underscored how serious the disruption had become.

When the world’s leading energy coordination body reaches for its biggest emergency tool, that does not communicate calm. It communicates that officials believe the threat to supply is real, immediate, and historically significant. Investors appeared to understand both sides of that message. Relief rallies occurred, but they were fragile because the very existence of the rescue measure validated the seriousness of the problem.

Investment implication: When policymakers deploy large emergency measures and the targeted market still refuses to stabilize, investors should assume the underlying disruption is being treated as structural or at least persistent rather than merely emotional.

Everything Else Traded in the Shadow of Energy

The rest of the week’s market story flowed from that geopolitical and energy backdrop. Inflation data, for instance, looked manageable on the surface, but the market treated it as stale almost immediately. February CPI was broadly in line with expectations, which ordinarily would have been mildly supportive for risk assets.

But investors quickly recognized that the report did not yet reflect the latest jump in fuel prices tied to the Iran conflict. That is why Thursday’s oil spike hit equities so hard: it threatened to change the inflation picture going forward even if the backward-looking data were acceptable.

That dynamic also helps explain sector behavior. Energy outperformed whenever crude surged, but many of the market’s sharpest losers were the industries most exposed to higher fuel costs or tighter financial conditions. Airlines, truckers, cruises, and homebuilders all felt that pressure, and that makes sense.

A higher oil price is not just an energy-sector positive; it is a cost shock for transportation, a margin squeeze for cyclicals, and a headwind to consumers already dealing with elevated borrowing costs. The market also became less confident about near-term Fed easing as oil rose, because a renewed inflation impulse from energy would make policymakers less eager to cut aggressively. Reuters reported the oil shock arrived just as core PCE remained firm, reinforcing the idea that the path to easier policy could become more complicated.

Technology, however, remained more nuanced. This week did not produce a simple “risk-off means tech down” pattern. Certain AI and semiconductor-linked names held up relatively well, and Oracle’s (ORCL) beat-and-raise served as a reminder that large-scale AI infrastructure spending is still a real earnings story, not just a thematic narrative.

But software remained more mixed, with Adobe (ADBE) hurt by its CEO transition and broader SaaS names struggling to sustain rebounds. In other words, even in a geopolitical macro storm, the market continued to separate AI infrastructure winners from software firms facing business-model pressure, leadership uncertainty, or valuation compression.

Chart of the Week

Market breadth has deteriorated throughout March as the escalation of the Iran conflict, the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, and the resulting surge in oil prices have weighed on equities.

The percentage of stocks trading above their 50-day moving average has fallen to roughly 32%, while the percentage of stocks trading above their 200-day moving average has declined to about 50%, levels now approaching the lows seen last November. From a technical standpoint, readings below 30% are typically considered oversold territory.

Breadth is only part of the picture, however. The recent pullback has produced broader technical damage across charts and sentiment indicators, including the breadth measures mentioned above. I will be publishing a dedicated technical analysis piece next week that examines the “weight of the evidence” approach - looking at multiple indicators together rather than relying on any single metric.

The charts below illustrate the recent deterioration in breadth across both the intermediate trend (percent of stocks above 50-day moving average) and the longer-term trend (percent of stocks above 200-day moving average). If the current weakness ultimately mirrors the November decline, the market may be approaching a potential buying window.

However, if the damage begins to resemble deeper corrections such as the April pullback or the 2022 bear market, the adjustment process may not yet be complete. In those scenarios, the percentage of stocks trading above their 200-day moving average would likely need to fall closer to 30% or below before reaching a stronger technical buy zone.

Source: StockCharts, Ryan Puplava, CMT® CTS™ CES™

Source: StockCharts, Ryan Puplava, CMT® CTS™ CES™

The dollar is currently testing key resistance and possibly forming an intermediate bottom. A breakout in the dollar could weigh on commodity prices such as precious metals, basic metals, agricultural, and regional energy prices not tied to the Strait of Hormuz situation like natural gas.

Source: StockCharts, Ryan Puplava, CMT® CTS™ CES™

Bottom Line

The broader takeaway from the week is that the geopolitical story was not merely an overlay on top of normal market action. It became the central organizing force. Oil, shipping, military posture, reserve releases, and the market’s assessment of whether the U.S. and its allies can actually restore secure transit through Hormuz dictated the path of equities more than any single data release or company report.

Until the market sees convincing evidence that shipping lanes are reopening sustainably and the risk of renewed disruption is falling, geopolitical headlines are likely to remain the first lens through which investors interpret everything else.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Spotify: Substantial User Growth Ahead Of Price Increases
data.description:
2026 is proving to be an incredibly choppy stock market, with AI fears mixing in with sharpening geopolitical tensions. Investors are looking for any and all reasons to sell off stocks; that said, it's a critical time for us to deploy active portfolio management and opportunistically buy high-quality companies on the dip.

Spotify (SPOT), in my view, is a prime example of a company enjoying substantial tailwinds for the year. The stock is down ~10% since the start of January, though a recent strong Q4 earnings print that featured big beats on monthly active users and Premium subscribers helped to tilt sentiment upward.

I last wrote a buy rating on Spotify in January, when the stock was trading at $508 per share. Since then, Spotify has seen its shares erode somewhat, even though the company has achieved accelerating revenue growth amid a burst of new users. Amid price increases set to take effect in the U.S. in FY26, Spotify is well positioned for continued success, and I'm reiterating my buy rating on the stock.

In my view, these are the core tailwinds that Spotify enjoys:

  • Price increases baked in for 2026. Spotify's base U.S. prices increased from $11.99 to $12.99 in February. On top of this, many of the company's European markets also bumped up by €1 in the tail end of FY25. Spotify is effectively enjoying a full year of price increases in most major markets in FY26, with very easy comps (not comping against the Europe price increase until the end of FY26).
  • Spotify has proven itself to be very sticky. The company has been bold in continuing to increase prices because it has proven that its Premium subscriptions have very high retention. Spotify users remain on the platform thanks to the stickiness of its playlists and the breadth of its offerings (spanning podcasts as well).
  • Scaling the ads business. As the company's ad-supported MAUs continue to build up, the company's pro forma gross margins in the ad-supported business as also improving, despite near-term weakness in ad budgets.
  • Plenty of greenfield opportunity to continue expanding. Though Spotify already feels like a global, monolithic brand, the company notes that only 3.5% of the world's population are Spotify subscribers. The company continues to view 10-15% of the global population as a reasonable target for long-term expansion.

Stay long here: in my view, Spotify is well positioned to continue rallying.

Q4 download

Let's now go through Spotify's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Spotify's revenue grew 7% y/y to €4.53 billion, ahead of Wall Street's expectations of €4.52 billion. Revenue from Premium subscribers grew at an 8% y/y pace, relatively flat to 9% growth in Q3, while declines in ad-supported revenue of -4% y/y softened from -6% last quarter.

The company executed its best-ever Wrapped campaign in December, which contributed to user outperformance. As shown in the chart below, Spotify's monthly active users grew 11% y/y to 751 million, adding 37 million sequential MAUs within the quarter. We note as well that the company's 290 million Premium subscribers (adding 9 million net-new Premium subs within the quarter) exceeded guidance calling for 289 million subs. Again, this is in spite of the €1 price increase in many European markets in late Q3/early Q4, which is a good indicator for user retention ahead of the broader U.S. price increase in Q1.

Speaking to healthy user adds and engagement during the quarter on the Q4 earnings call, co-CEO Alex Norström noted as follows:

In Q4, we met or exceeded guidance across all the key metrics. We marked our highest quarter ever for MAU net additions. It's just incredible to think that we now serve over 0.75 billion people around the world. Since going public, I have been touting the importance of our flywheel, and it all starts with MAU growth, which in turn fuels the growth of our overall business.

A driver of MAU outperformance is wrapped, which was also record-breaking this year. While we saw impressive engagement back in 2024, we also got feedback on the user experience. So this year, we turned up the dial and the response was redeeming. At the end of the campaign, more than 300 million users engaged, which was up 20%, and we saw more than 630 million shares across social media, which is up 42%. Even more, day 1 of Wrapped marked the highest single day of subscriber intake in Spotify history."

We note that Spotify is calling for continued user strength heading into Q1, with its outlook guiding to 8 million net-new MAU additions in Q1 (versus only 3 million added in Q1'25) and 3 million total Premium subscriber adds. Q1 performance will be key to monitoring how U.S. retention trends after the $12.99 price kicks in.

Meanwhile, on the profitability front, Spotify's pro forma gross margins improved 80 bps y/y to 33.1%, driven by economies of scale on the ad-supported business, while operating margins jumped 430 bps y/y to 15.5%.

The company is also becoming a free cash flow powerhouse, with FCF of €2.87 billion growing 26% y/y and hitting a 17% margin against revenue.

Valuation and key takeaways

Spotify has long been a growth stock that traded at a premium to other internet peers, but amid this year's share price compression, its bottom-line valuation multiples have also compressed closer in line to peers. As shown below, Spotify trades at a 26x forward adjusted EBITDA multiple, which is about ~10% richer than its closest pure-play peer, Netflix (NFLX).

That said, we do note the fact that Spotify is still relatively early in its profit generation. With a rising gross margin profile and rapidly growing cash flow, we can look instead to Spotify's revenue multiple, which is significantly below internet peers, to help justify Spotify's valuation.

With price increases set to kick in this year on top of already-strong user trends, Spotify remains a compelling buy.

data.title: XBI: Biotech M&A Has Been Hot, But Price Trends Have Cooled
data.description:
Biotech stocks were on a heater coming off the April 2025 lows. The State Street SPDR S&P Biotech ETF (XBI) sharply outperformed the S&P 500 Trust ETF (SPY) and the State Street Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLV) over the back half of last year, but its absolute and relative strength have waned notably in the past few months.

Higher interest rates and a less risk-on market have been headwinds. What’s more, the IPO pipeline has eased, although healthcare M&A remains stout. So, it’s not all negative for the equal-weight biotech fund.

I had a hold rating on XBI back in May 2025, and I reiterate that today. The valuation appears fair, while the technicals point to the potential for further downside as we progress through a sometimes-tough seasonal stretch.

XBI Soared in 2H25, But Has Cooled YTD

North American M&A Has Climbed

According to the issuer, XBI seeks to provide exposure to the Biotechnology segment of the S&P Total Market Index, which comprises the following sub-industries: Biotechnology. It follows a modified equal-weighted index, which offers the potential for unconcentrated industry exposure across large-, mid-, and small-cap stocks, allowing investors to take strategic or tactical positions at a more targeted level than traditional sector-based investing.

XBI is a medium-sized ETF with $7.8 billion in assets under management as of March 12, 2026. That’s significantly above the AUM from the time of my previous analysis in Q2 2025 ($4.9 billion). Its annual expense ratio is moderate at 35 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is low at only 0.36%, about 0.7 percentage points below that of the S&P 500.

Share-price momentum is rated very well by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system, but I take issue with its strength today—more on that to come at the conclusion of the article. From a risk perspective, XBI can swing wildly, as evidenced by a high historical realized standard deviation percentage and its bent to domestic small caps.

Finally, on liquidity, XBI scores well. Average daily volume is now north of 9 million shares, while the median 30-day bid/ask spread is just two basis points, per State Street Funds.

For an update on the portfolio, the 2-star, Bronze-rated ETF by Morning is centered on the bottom-right corner of the style box, indicating its high weight to US small-cap growth. That spot is not only susceptible to swings in the macroeconomy, but there’s also a needed growth narrative required for XBI to work. Often, that’s dealmaking and favorable regulatory developments in the biotech space. We have seen that to an extent this year, but perhaps 2025 pulled forward the equity gains.

As it stands, XBI trades with a 19.3x price-to-earnings ratio—about three figures above the P/E from last May. Moreover, the long-term EPS growth rate is modest at only 3.2%. So, it’s not a compelling value either on a P/E or PEG basis right now.

XBI: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

Turning to the holding profile, the 100% Health Care product is well diversified from a single-stock perspective. In all, the top 10 equities account for a very low 16.2% of XBI. I’d also call out that the ETF paid out its highest total dividend in the last 10 years in 2025.

While that may be encouraging, it could also be a sign that there are fewer growth opportunities for today’s biotech leaders.

XBI: Holdings & Dividend Information

Seasonally, there’s not a lot to be excited about, either. March has been far and away the worst month in price data over the past decade, but historically, better trends have developed as the second quarter plays out. So, this indicator augurs for a wait-and-see approach.

XBI: Soft Near-Term Calendar Trends

The Technical Take

With a lackluster valuation and dicey near-term calendar trends, XBI’s technical situation is mixed. Notice in the chart below that the ETF may be putting in a bearish rounded top pattern. That’s fine for now, but if XBI breaks key support near the $118-$120 zone, then a measured move downside price target to about $106 is in play based on the height of the recent range. $106 is also where the late 2024 highs come into view.

Also take a look at the long-term 200-day moving average. It’s on the rise, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend. It, too, is close to $106. Adding to the significance of that spot, the 38.2% Fibonacci retracement of the April 2025 to early 2026 rally hits there. So, buying in the $106 to $110 zone appears as a favorable risk/reward strategy.

Finally, the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph printed a series of lower highs as price rallied—that portended the current topping pattern.

XBI: Bearish Rounded Top, $106 Support Confluence, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a hold rating on XBI. Buying on a pullback to $106-$110 may be the strategy here, particularly as more favorable seasonal trends could get going toward mid-year.

data.title: Q4 GDP Second Estimate: Real GDP At 0.7%, Lower Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

U.S. economic growth cooled more than expected in Q4 2025, according to the BEA’s latest estimate. Real GDP rose at just a 0.7% annual rate, falling well short of the 1.4% forecast and marking a steep drop-off from the 4.4% final estimate seen in Q3.

Here is the opening text from the Bureau of Economic Analysis news release:

Real gross domestic product (GDP) increased at an annual rate of 0.7 percent in the fourth quarter of 2025 (October, November, and December), according to the second estimate released today by the U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis. In the third quarter, real GDP increased 4.4 percent. The contributors to the increase in real GDP in the fourth quarter were increases in consumer spending and investment. These movements were partly offset by decreases in government spending and exports. Imports, which are a subtraction in the calculation of GDP, decreased.

Gross Domestic Product (GDP)

Real gross domestic product (GDP) measures how fast or slowly the economy is growing and measures the inflation-adjusted value of all goods and services produced by the economy. It is considered the broadest measure of economic activity and the primary indicator of an economy's health.

The Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA) releases real GDP data on a monthly basis. There are three versions released a month apart - advance, advance, second, and final - each incorporating data that was previously unavailable. Economists can use GDP to determine whether an economy is growing or experiencing a recession.

This chart illustrates the annualized, inflation-adjusted change in U.S. gross domestic product (GDP) from the preceding quarter, with data beginning in Q2 1947. (Prior to Q2 1947, GDP was calculated annually.) For context, the chart also highlights recessions (as determined by the National Bureau of Economic Research, NBER), the historical GDP growth average of 3.19%, and the current 10-year moving average of 2.72%.

A key trend is evident: the 10-year moving average of GDP growth fell below the historical average in 2007 and has remained there, signaling a significant deceleration in U.S. economic expansion since the Great Recession. Current growth is below both the long-term series average and the 10-year moving average, indicating a slower pace of growth than experienced historically.

The chart above has many interesting data points but can be quite overwhelming to look at. This next chart provides a simplified look over the past few years.

Here is a log-scale chart of real GDP with an exponential regression, which helps us understand growth cycles since the 1947 inception of quarterly GDP. The regression illustrates the fact that the current trend, since the Great Recession, has a visibly lower slope than the long-term trend. In fact, the current GDP is 12.6% below the pre-recession trend (2008).

A particularly telling representation of slowing growth in the US economy is the year-over-year rate of change. Beginning in 1947, the average year-over-year rate at the start of recessions is 3.2%, with a range of 1.28% to 6.78%. The current year-over-year rate for real GDP is at 2.03%. The current level is at or below 10 of the 12 recession starts during this time frame.

GDP: A Look Ahead

In the chart below, we use the Conference Board's Economic Forecast for the U.S. Economy to visualize GDP forecasts over the next few years.

March 11, 2026

Iran War to Test Consumer Endurance

The war in Iran is likely to weigh on consumer spending in 2026. Our models of the impact of the recent spike in oil prices show lower GDP growth, higher inflation, and as a result tougher choices for the Fed.

Before the start of the war, consumers were already under pressure as 2025 tariffs continued to pass into retail prices. An additional tax in the form of higher prices at the pump will likely dent consumer spending elsewhere. The recent spike in policy uncertainty due to the war may prevent companies from raising headcounts. This would still nudge the unemployment rate higher in the coming months, even as companies abstain from laying off workers. This in turn could further cap personal income growth and restrain economic performance in 2026.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: 8%+ Monthly Dividend And Long-Term Outperformance: Analyzing RQI After The Dip
data.description:
The Cohen & Steers Quality Income Realty Fund Inc (RQI) has recently dipped by about 6%, pushing the dividend yield up to around 8.7%, putting an end to what had previously been a very strong rally since the end of last year.

We last covered it as an attractive option in 2024 when the Fed was pivoting towards rate cuts. While that bullish take failed for reasons we recently detailed here, in this article, we will take a closer look at this closed-end fund to see if it is worth buying on the dip.

RQI: A Proven Track Record Of Success

RQI has a lot going for it. First of all, it is an actively managed real estate closed-end fund that has a phenomenal track record. It has outperformed the Morningstar USCEF real estate index on a three-month, six-month, one-year, three-year, five-year, and ten-year basis, meaning that it is a consistent outperformer.

This is also evidenced by comparing it to the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ) over the long term, which shows that RQI has substantially outperformed it since its inception as well.

Thus, the active management seems to be quite skilled at adding value to shareholders over time.

Additionally, it has proven to be a very stable dividend payer, as it pays out an attractive monthly distribution and continues to do so even through the COVID-19 lockdowns. In fact, it recently paid a substantial special dividend at the end of 2025 and into early 2026 and also hiked the monthly dividend from 8 cents per share to 9 cents per share, which amounts to a 12.5% increase. For a CEF that already yields about 8.7%, that is a pretty substantial dividend hike.

Valuation And Portfolio Considerations

Meanwhile, it currently trades at around a 2% discount to its net asset value, which is a pretty attractive discount for a fund that has outperformed over the long term. That being said, it does not look particularly cheap given where the 52-week range is, with the 52-week low being at a 7.77% discount, the 52-week high being just a 1.03% premium, and the 52-week average being at a 3.83% discount to that asset value.

Perhaps the biggest consideration and potential weakness of the fund is that it has substantial leverage to the tune of over 29% at the moment. Additionally, it has a pretty high expense ratio that, when including the interest expense for its leverage, comes in at about 3.45%.

Nonetheless, its long-term total return performance indicates that it is more than earning the fees it charges to investors, thereby making that justified. Its stable distributions over time also mean that it has managed to handle its leverage well during periods of volatility.

In terms of its actual portfolio, it is pretty heavily overweight in a couple of high-conviction names. As of the end of 2025, it has:

  • A 16.26% allocation to Welltower (WELL).
  • A 9.28% allocation to Digital Realty Trust (DLR).
  • An 8.64% allocation to American Tower (AMT).
  • A 7.03% allocation to Crown Castle (CCI).
  • A 6.98% allocation to Prologis (PLD).

Along with a few other high-conviction bets. This means that the diversification is not as great as its 202 total holdings seem to indicate. At the same time, you are paying them a fee for active management, so it is good to see that they are not simply trying to closet index.

Macro Outlook For Real Estate

Thus, RQI appears to be a skillfully managed leveraged bet on the REIT sector. Since the Fed began hiking rates in early 2022, REITs have massively underperformed the broader market.

Meanwhile, they have continued to strengthen their balance sheets, and many have continued to display solid fundamentals. Thus, I think that they remain a relatively attractive place to allocate capital today, even after their recent rally and the recent dip, which helps to make the sector look even more attractive.

Additionally, I expect the ongoing dollarization trend, which I recently detailed here, to fuel a fresh drive into investment-grade real assets in the United States, of which real estate is an obvious component. Thus, I think that RQI should benefit from some long-term macro tailwinds moving forward.

Of course, the other side of it is that the war in Iran has caused oil prices to spike, which in turn raises inflationary fears and in turn should push interest rates higher, at least in the near term. This is what has driven the recent pullback in the sector, and it could be a headwind for at least the medium term. Thus, for investors looking for a quick leveraged bid higher, I think you can do better than RQI. However, if you are a long-term investor, the recent dip creates another buying opportunity in a high-quality CEF that is now trading at a discount to net asset value.

While I am personally finding better individual REIT picks in my actively managed 7-8% yielding and total return outperforming portfolio at High Yield Investor, for investors who prefer to keep it simple and trust an active manager to do their work for them, RQI is one of the better options right now.

data.title: NLY-F Preferred: A 5% Spread Over T-Bills With Floating Income
data.description:
Annaly Capital Management’s 6.95% Series F Fixed-to-Floating Preferred (NLY.PR.F) has a current yield of 8.8%. That figure is high enough to make it unusual among most investment-grade preferred securities. Just as a point of reference, iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF (PFF) is around ~5.8%, so NLY-F’s yield is around 300 bps above that benchmark.

However, even more than that yield, what's unusual is that NLY-F is a floating rate coupon that resets quarterly. It's linked to SOFR through a precise reset formula:

…three-month CME Term SOFR (plus a spread adjustment of 0.26161%) on the Dividend Determination date plus a spread of 4.993%.

This comes to SOFR + 5.25461% and that link to SOFR makes it a floating rate credit instrument, much different from a conventional perpetual preferred.

Another positive is that NLY-F is a cumulative preferred, meaning if dividends are unpaid, they accrue. This gives investors added protection in the unlikely event of a dividend non-payment, something that non‑cumulative bank preferreds do not offer.

A third positive is that given its floating structure, NLY-F has much less duration risk compared to fixed-rate preferreds. Those stay highly sensitive to changes in T-yields and typically behave like perpetual bonds, often with durations as long as 15-20 years. NLY-F, on the other hand, behaves more like a short-duration credit instrument, avoiding large price drops during rising rate cycles. When rates rise, NLY-F adjusts mostly by raising dividends, not by cutting prices.

NLY-F’s seniority is the fourth positive aspect I want to highlight. Within Annaly’s capital stack, NLY-F sits just below repo but above Annaly’s large $16 billion common equity base. This equity base absorbs the losses first, acting as a cushion for the preferreds. From time to time, when the market conditions are good, Annaly raises equity capital, which adds to the cushion.

The Floating Coupon Mechanics

NLY-F’s floating coupon resets every quarter based on a formula tied to the three-month Term SOFR plus a fixed margin. In the table below, you can see what happens to the coupon at different SOFR levels.

SOFR level

Implied coupon on par

5.00%

~10.25%

3.00%

~8.25%

1.00%

~6.25%

As we can see, the fixed margin of 5.25% (after adjustments) helps stabilize the coupon. The current SOFR is 3.65%, which gives us the current coupon at ~8.93% (rounded). If the SOFR rises to 5%, the coupon goes to ~10.25%. That's fine, but what's important to know is that, even if the rates normalize to something realistic, like 3%, the coupon is still a respectable ~8.25%. Indeed, even in an extreme scenario when the SOFR drops to 1% or less (this happened during the pandemic), that margin still keeps the implied coupon at a decent ~6.25%.

Capital Structure and Preferred Coverage

Mortgage REITs rely on high leverage, funding their portfolios typically with short-term repurchase agreements. These sit at the top of the capital stack as secured financing.

On the liability side, Annaly has around $81.9 billion of repo agreements outstanding. This is very high leverage in dollar terms, however, its reported economic leverage ratio was ~5.5x, which is moderate by historical mREIT standards. That figure comes from the repo borrowings plus other secured borrowings, forward MSR exposures, and derivatives, which together add up to $90.91 billion of economic debt, divided by the $16.16 billion of total equity.

Annaly has kept its preferreds protected in various ways. During stress periods, it has cut common dividends or reduced leverage. Whenever market conditions have been favorable, NLY has raised new common equity capital to strengthen its equity cushion. They raised $2.9 billion in 2025.

Funding Structure and Liquidity Risk

Annaly’s primary risk is its reliance on large short-term funding. These repo borrowings are secured by mortgage-backed securities used as collateral. The maturity date is usually short: Annaly reported that weighted average days to maturity for repurchase agreements fell to 35 days in Q4 2025 (from 49 days in the prior quarter), implying the repo book is effectively refinanced ~10 times per year.

The difference between the yield on their mortgage assets and the cost of the repo funding they use to finance them is the spread that generates Annaly’s income. During stress periods, this same structure may create liquidity risks. During these times, repo lenders may tighten collateral requirements. They may even reduce financing broadly, and mREITs like NLY are then forced to sell assets to raise cash to meet margin calls and repay repo funding.

Annaly adopts several measures to reduce this risk. First, it borrows from a diversified group of lenders, or repo counterparties. This includes major broker-dealers and commercial banks. Second, Annaly borrows across multiple maturity durations. This avoids the need for large refinancing at any one period. Third, it keeps a modest leverage profile. That 5.5x figure I quoted earlier takes into account hedging and other debt, so it is a broader measure - and is modest compared to historical mREIT leverage figures.

Among other measures, Annaly maintains large liquidity buffers. So they reported ~$9.4 billion of “assets available for financing,” including about $6.1 billion of cash and unencumbered agency MBS totaling ~$7.8 billion. These securities are not currently pledged for repo collateral, and Annaly can use them to quickly raise cash or meet margin calls.

Lastly, Annaly uses active hedging, around $81 billion in all, with a hedge ratio of 90%. This helps offset changes in mortgage asset prices when interest rates rise, and reduces sudden margin calls when markets are stressed.

Portfolio diversification and the credit/MSR hedge

Annaly’s very diverse portfolio also stabilizes risk. At the end of 2025, it had 62% agency MBS, 19% residential credit and another 19% mortgage servicing rights - MSR. In dollar terms, this corresponded to about $92.9 billion of agency assets, an $8 billion residential credit portfolio, and roughly $3.8 billion of MSR market value.

MSRs provide a good hedge in higher-rate environments. Here, Annaly earns a fee for managing mortgage loans - typically 25-50 basis points of the outstanding loan balance each year. When interest rates rise, homeowners do not refinance as much, because they probably have a lower-rate loan than what they can get now. When refinancing slows, the underlying mortgage loan runs for longer periods. Since MSRs earn fees based on the outstanding each year, they earn longer when the loan runs longer. So when interest rates rise and other income streams decline, MSR actually improves - providing a much-needed hedge.

Annaly’s MSR book is positioned for the current “low refi” environment. At the end of 2025 the company serviced around $242.8 billion of UPB (unpaid principal balance), with an average mortgage rate of about 3.28%. Their three-month CPR (Conditional Prepayment Rate) was ~4.6%, a historically low number indicating very low refinancing. These figures support continuing servicing cash flows as well as how its MSRs play their hedging role in a high rate environment. There are several credit quality metrics where Annaly’s MSR portfolio does well, but I'm not cluttering the discussion with FICO and LTV figures because the MSR story here is not about credit losses, but prepayment behavior.

Annaly’s preferreds do a good balancing act between the pros and cons of mortgage REITs. While these REITs produce strong yields, they're prone to occasional crisis situations, not because of credit losses, but because of liquidity problems in the market. Annaly's moderate leverage profile and its diversified funding with hedging reduce that probability. The preferreds offer an additional layer of security, very attractive for shareholders, in that Annaly’s large equity capital works as a cushion for preferred shareholders.

Annaly’s preferred stack consists of Series F, G, I and J, and together they add up to ~$1.802 billion, so their equity stack is 9 times the preferreds. This gives us an idea of the size of the buffer. When losses happen, they must first pass through this large equity base before they can impact the preferreds. That said, major funding shocks do generate severe mark-to-market volatility in the entire capital stack.

Call Optionality and Rate Scenarios

Just like most other preferred securities, Annaly can use a call option on NLY-F and redeem the shares at their $25 liquidation price. When short-term rates fall sharply, Annaly’s new financing cost can become less than the current coupon cost (because it is tied to the SOFR). This would give Annaly the financial incentive to redeem the preferreds. When this happens, NLY-F’s price can move closer to or even above $25, its par value.

Now, there are two points to note here. The first idea is that Series F has been callable since September 2022. A new buyer will see a small capital loss if Annaly redeems now, when it is trading 30 cents above par. This loss should, however, be adjusted against accrued dividends.

The other point is that, given the current floating coupon of ~8.93%, refinancing costs must be below 8% for the savings to appeal to Annaly. Here are a few scenarios:

Scenario

Coupon

Annual cost on $1.8B preferred stack

Current NLY-F

~8.93%

~$161M

Refinance at 8.0%

~$144M

Savings

~$17M per year

This means investors continue to earn a high dividend while the rates stay elevated. If they fall sharply, shares could be redeemed at $25 so Annaly can lower their cost of capital. This way, the call option caps upside. The downside risk stays small. For example, since shares trade at just 30 cents above par today, investors potentially see a “call-at-par” loss of just 1-1.2%. Again, the dividend income offsets this small loss.

Now, short-term rates were ~3.7% as of early March, so NLY-F’s yield is ~5.1 percentage points above three-month Treasuries. This is a very large premium, and accounts for most of the income. Investors demand this premium as compensation for the risk they take in buying the preferreds over the safer T-bills. Unlike the latter, Annaly uses leverage through repo funding. That, plus the mortgage spread volatility, constitutes the risk.

Conclusion

Annaly’s Series F preferred comes with a high yield tied to the SOFR. Annaly’s large equity base works as a cushion, absorbing losses first. The leverage profile is moderate, and their mortgage platform is diversified. The MSR portfolio acts as a hedge when rates change, improving the stability of earnings.

While these things do not completely eliminate the risks inherent in mREITs, the preferred stack comes with lower risk due to the equity buffer. Investors may find this attractive, even if they're paying slightly above par. They can lock in the dividends immediately and consider adding aggressively when the price falls below par.
